nativeRange.habitat: Brackish mangrove estuaries, tidal rivers, coastal lagoons, and the lower reaches of freshwater streams

diet.notes: In the wild, the Archerfish targets terrestrial and aquatic invertebrates by spitting jets of water to knock them from overhanging vegetation. In captivity it tends to accept floating pellets, freeze-dried insects, live or frozen insects such as crickets, mealworms, cockroaches, and flies, as well as frozen bloodworm and brine shrimp. Hand-feeding insects held above the water surface is a common enrichment practice that encourages the species' natural spitting behaviour, and offering live insects several times weekly is generally recommended.

goodWith: other robust brackish species of similar size
avoidWith: small fish that may be eaten

summary: The Archerfish is a brackish-water specialist from Indo-Pacific mangroves, best known for knocking insects off overhanging vegetation by spitting jets of water up to 2 metres. Best kept in groups of four or more in a large brackish aquarium with a secure lid.
careNotes: Brackish water — a mix of fresh and salt water, with a specific gravity (a measure of dissolved salt) between 1.005 and 1.015 — is generally considered essential for the Archerfish's long-term health. Pure freshwater has been reported to cause chronic stress, reduced immunity, and a shortened lifespan. Groups of four or more tend to display more natural behaviour and reduce sparring between individuals, though larger fish may monopolise feeding if there is a significant size difference within the group. A tightly sealed lid is essential: archerfish are documented jumpers and will readily launch water at overhead lights or decorations. Leaving 15–20 cm of air space between the water surface and the lid is recommended. Overhanging live or artificial vegetation encourages natural spitting behaviour. Water quality should be maintained at a high standard through strong biological filtration and regular partial water changes. The Archerfish is sometimes confused in trade with T. chatareus (the Clouded Archerfish, which bears smaller, less defined spots) and T. microlepis (the Smallscale Archerfish, which has banded rather than spotted markings); care requirements across Toxotes species are broadly similar. No confirmed captive breeding has been documented, and wild-caught specimens remain the norm in the trade.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding has not been documented. Spawning is believed to require higher-salinity or fully marine conditions — conditions that are difficult or impossible to replicate in a home aquarium — which may explain the absence of captive reproduction records. Reliable methods for distinguishing males from females are not known. The Archerfish is generally considered not reproducible in home aquaria, and all specimens in the trade are wild-caught.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish
primary.notes: Primary source; used for size, temperature, pH, tank dimensions, diet, temperament, and native range. Aquarium Co-Op (slice primary) has no dedicated Toxotes jaculatrix care page.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (30 cm TL), temperature (25–30°C), and habitat description (brackish mangrove estuaries). Confirmed family Toxotidae.

additional.name: Gensou Premium Aquascaping
additional.notes: Cross-checked size (20–25 cm), temperature (24–28°C), pH (7.5–8.0), gH (12–20 dGH), salinity (SG 1.005–1.010), min tank (300L+), lifespan (up to 10 years), escape risk (high), schooling (groups of 4–6).

additional.name: AquaSnack
additional.notes: Cross-checked temperature (24–28°C), pH (7.5–8.5), salinity (SG 1.005–1.015), tank size (55–100 gal / ~208–380L), diet, and escape risk (high). Confirmed brackish requirement.

fish.breedingNotes: No confirmed captive breeding; believed to require higher-salinity conditions for spawning. All traded specimens are wild-caught.
